issues.title: Jimmy Clay & Roy Kizer Golf Course Rezoning Initiation
issues.summary:
Resolution initiating rezoning of approximately 445 acres of City-owned land (Jimmy Clay and Roy Kizer golf courses) at 5400 Jimmy Clay Drive. Sponsored by CMs Fuentes, Alter, Ellis, and Laine. This could be one of the largest single housing development opportunities in Austin if the land is eventually rezoned for residential/mixed-use.

**June 3 update:** Council in summer recess until July 23. The density bonus program (DBC) approved May 22 and the Dog's Head annexation (2,600 acres) approved the same day show strong council pro-development trajectory. Council also approved new development rules on May 26: minimum lot size reduced from 5,750 to 1,800 sq ft citywide — the most significant lot size change in 80 years. Missing middle zoning districts and new mixed-use zoning districts also created. These reforms complement the golf course rezoning opportunity.

**June 5 update:** Council on summer recess until July 23. Bond direction ($390M, final vote July 23) does not include housing. Development rules overhaul in implementation phase. Monitor for golf course rezoning hearing dates when council returns.

**June 19 update:** Juneteenth — city offices CLOSED. Rezoning process continues for 445-acre Jimmy Clay/Roy Kizer site (5,000-15,000 unit potential). Council on recess until July 23. ⚡ AISD budget approved 7-1 at $205M — school closures in south Austin could accelerate demand for family housing on this site.

**June 20 update:** Rezoning process continues for 445-acre Jimmy Clay/Roy Kizer site (5,000-15,000 unit potential). Council on recess until July 23. AISD budget approved at $205M — south Austin school closures and boundary realignment (workshops June 22-23) could reshape demand patterns for family housing on this site.

**June 21 update:** Rezoning process continues for 445-acre Jimmy Clay/Roy Kizer site (5,000-15,000 unit potential). Council resolution sponsored by Fuentes, Alter, Ellis, and Laine. AISD boundary workshops TOMORROW: June 22 and 23 — south Austin school consolidation could reshape housing demand for this site. Bond survey closes June 23 (2 DAYS). Council on recess until July 23 (32 DAYS).

**June 22 update:** AISD boundary workshops TODAY (10-11:30 AM) and tomorrow (6-7:30 PM) — two draft maps 'Oak' and 'Elm' show different elementary-to-middle-to-high pathways after 10 school closures. Online comment card open through July 31. Revised draft map to trustees in August; board vote expected September; implementation August 2027. South Austin school consolidation could reshape housing demand for this 445-acre site. Bond survey closes TOMORROW (1 DAY). Council on recess until July 23 (31 DAYS). Filing period for council races opens July 19 (27 DAYS).

**June 23 update:** ⚡ Austin 2026 Bond community survey CLOSED TODAY (June 23). Over 2,000 participants shared 53,000+ individual responses including 1,637 open-ended comments. Top priorities: transportation and housing/homelessness tied as greatest needs, followed by parks, public safety, flood protection, and climate investments. 70%+ of responses said climate and sustainability considerations are 'very or somewhat important' for all bond projects. City staff will compile results and present final ~$390M bond package to council when they return from recess July 23 (30 DAYS). AISD virtual boundary workshop TONIGHT (6-7:30 PM) — second of two sessions on 'Oak' and 'Elm' draft maps showing school pathways after 10 closures. Online comment card open through July 31. Draft boundary changes to trustees in August; board vote September. Filing period for council races opens July 19 (26 DAYS).

AISD boundary workshops completed — south Austin school consolidation reshapes housing demand for this site. Bond survey closed — staff compiling results. Council on recess until July 23 (29 DAYS). Filing period for D1 opens July 19 (25 DAYS).

**June 25 update:** Rezoning process continues for 445-acre Jimmy Clay/Roy Kizer site (5,000-15,000 unit potential). Council resolution sponsored by Fuentes, Alter, Ellis, and Laine. **June 26 update:** Rezoning process continues for 445-acre Jimmy Clay/Roy Kizer site (5,000-15,000 unit potential). Council resolution sponsored by Fuentes, Alter, Ellis, and Laine. public_comment.instructions: This was an initiation resolution — the actual rezoning process will have extensive public hearings. Monitor for future hearing dates.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: 445 acres of potential housing on city-owned land is a massive pro-development opportunity. Aligns directly with your YIMBY values. The initiation vote signals council intent; the actual rezoning fights will come later and will need organized pro-housing voices.

issues.title: Convention Center Redevelopment — Petition Rejected by Court
issues.summary:
UPDATE (Feb 2026): Travis County District Judge Jessica Mangrum sided with Austin and upheld the City Clerk's rejection of the Austin United PAC petition to halt the $1.6 billion convention center redevelopment. The PAC had collected 25,000+ signatures but the Clerk's office certified it as ~500 signatures short. After a two-day trial in late January, the court ruled for the city. Mayor Watson called it 'a good win for Austinites.' The PAC has announced plans to organize a new petition targeting the November 2026 ballot. Construction on the new complex continues — expected to open by late 2028 with ~620,000 sq ft of rentable space (70% increase over the old center).

**June 7 update:** The Texas Supreme Court denied Austin United's appeal on April 2, exhausting the PAC's legal options to stop the $1.6B redevelopment through the courts. The PAC is now organizing a new citizen-led petition campaign to place the issue directly before voters on the November 2026 ballot. Construction continues — the new convention center is expected to open late 2028 with approximately 620,000 sq ft of rentable space (70% increase over old center).

**June 20 update:** Construction continues on the $1.6B convention center — on track for late 2028 opening with ~620,000 sq ft of rentable space (70% increase). TX Supreme Court denied Austin United PAC's appeal April 2, exhausting legal options. PAC organizing new petition for November 2026 ballot. Council on recess until July 23.

**June 21 update:** ⚡ MAJOR LEGAL VICTORY: Travis County District Court Judge Sherine Thomas ruled June 18 that Austin's $1.35B bond funding plan for the convention center expansion is lawful. The city can use hotel occupancy tax funds to pay down debt on up to $1.35B in bonds approved by Council last month. Construction continues on the $1.6B project — excavation in full force, reaching depths of 50+ feet for underground exhibit halls and loading docks; 293,000 cubic yards of dirt removed. Reopening target: spring 2029, nearly doubling rentable space to ~620,000 sq ft. Austin United PAC's TX Supreme Court appeal denied April 2 — PAC now organizing NEW petition for November 2026 ballot. Council on recess until July 23 (32 DAYS).

**June 22 update:** Diaphragm wall (D-wall) construction underway — trenching in Area E (east side, between 2nd & 3rd Streets), concrete pours and rebar cages being installed. ⚡ Judge Thomas ruled June 18 that the $1.35B bond funding plan is lawful — city can use hotel occupancy tax funds to pay down debt. On track for spring 2029 reopening, nearly doubling rentable space to ~620,000 sq ft. Austin United PAC organizing new petition for November 2026 ballot. Council on recess until July 23 (31 DAYS).

**June 23 update:** D-wall construction continues in Area E. Bond survey closed today — convention center expansion NOT part of the ~$390M bond direction; funded separately via hotel occupancy tax per Judge Thomas's June 18 ruling. Construction on track for spring 2029 reopening. Austin United PAC organizing new petition for November ballot. Council on recess until July 23 (30 DAYS).

**June 24 update:** D-wall construction continues in Area E — trenching between 2nd & 3rd Streets, concrete pours and rebar cages being installed. Judge Thomas's June 18 ruling affirmed the $1.35B bond funding plan as lawful. On track for spring 2029 reopening, nearly doubling rentable space to ~620,000 sq ft. Austin United PAC organizing new petition for November 2026 ballot. Council on recess until July 23 (29 DAYS).

**June 25 update:** Excavation in full force — continuing through August 2026; 293,000+ cubic yards of dirt removed. D-wall construction progressing between 2nd & 3rd Streets. Judge Thomas's June 18 ruling affirmed the $1.35B bond funding plan. On track for spring 2029 reopening as world's first Net Zero Carbon Certified Convention Center — nearly doubling rentable space to ~620,000 sq ft. Austin United PAC organizing new petition for November 2026 ballot. Council on recess until July 23 (28 DAYS).

**June 26 update:** ⚡ Structural steel positioning beginning this month — major construction milestone. D-wall construction progressing between 2nd & 3rd Streets. Excavation continues through August 2026; 293,000+ cubic yards removed. TX Supreme Court denied Austin United petition April 2; June 18 bond ruling permanently enjoins future legal challenges to $1.35B bond funding. On track for spring 2029 reopening — nearly doubling rentable space to ~620,000 sq ft. Council on recess until July 23 (27 DAYS).

**June 27 update:** ⚡ D-wall construction COMPLETE — reinforced concrete perimeter structure finished, providing earth retention and foundation support for below-grade facilities. First footings poured. Structural steel positioning underway. 80 steel trusses salvaged from demolished building (548,000 lbs/274 tons) — 10 for reuse in Neches Pavilion, 45 for new warehouse. Low-carbon structural steel and rebar sourced from Nucor Texas (Jewett, TX). Steel package purchased early as cost-saving measure — proved prescient given subsequent tariff threats. Excavation continues through August 2026. On track for spring 2029 reopening — first zero-carbon-certified convention center in the world; LEED v4 Gold (Platinum target). Council on recess until July 23 (26 DAYS).

**June 28 update:** Excavation continues through August 2026. Structural steel positioning underway following D-wall completion; first footings poured. Low-carbon structural steel and rebar sourced from Nucor Texas (Jewett, TX) — early purchase proved prescient given tariff threats. 80 salvaged steel trusses (274 tons) — 10 for Neches Pavilion reuse, 45 for new warehouse. On track for spring 2029 reopening — first zero-carbon-certified convention center worldwide; LEED v4 Gold (Platinum target). Austin United PAC organizing new petition for November ballot. Council on recess until July 23 (25 DAYS).

**June 29 update:** D-wall construction COMPLETE — first footings poured. Structural steel positioning in progress. Structural demolition 100% complete. Excavation in full force, continuing through August 2026; 293,000+ cubic yards removed. TX Supreme Court denied Austin United petition April 2; June 18 bond ruling permanently enjoins future legal challenges. Targeting LEED Gold certification. On track for spring 2029 reopening — nearly doubling rentable space to ~620,000 sq ft. Council on recess until July 23 (24 DAYS).

**June 30 update:** June 18 court ruling: Judge Sherine Thomas ruled Austin's financial plan lawful — permanently secured $1.35B bond funding via hotel occupancy tax. D-wall complete; first footings poured; structural steel positioning in progress. Excavation continuing through August 2026. Targeting LEED Gold certification. On track for spring 2029 reopening — nearly doubling rentable space to ~620,000 sq ft. Council on recess until July 23 (23 DAYS).

**July 1 update:** Structural demolition 100% complete. D-wall complete; first footings poured; excavation continues through 2026. Targeting LEED Gold and zero-carbon certification. June 18 court ruling permanently secured $1.35B bond funding via hotel occupancy tax. On track for spring 2029 reopening — nearly doubling rentable space from 365,000 to ~620,000 sq ft. Council on recess until July 23 (22 DAYS).

**July 2 update:** Total project cost: $1.6B (up from previously stated $1.35B in bonds). Expansion will produce over 1 million total sq ft. SXSW and other events using alternative venues through 2028. Planned as world's first zero-carbon certified convention center, running on 100% renewable energy. Excavation continues through 2026; completion target spring 2029. Council on recess until July 23 (21 DAYS).

**July 3 update:** July 4 holiday TOMORROW — construction pauses for holiday. Excavation continues through 2026. D-wall complete; first footings poured; structural steel positioning underway. Targeting LEED Gold and zero-carbon certification. On track for spring 2029 reopening — over 1 million total sq ft. Council on recess until July 23 (20 DAYS).

**July 4 update:** Independence Day — construction paused for holiday. Excavation continues through 2026. D-wall complete; first footings poured; structural steel positioning underway. Targeting LEED Gold and zero-carbon certification. On track for spring 2029 reopening — over 1 million total sq ft. Council on recess until July 23 (19 DAYS).

**July 5 update:** Extended holiday weekend — construction paused. $1.6B 'Unconventional ATX' project: 293,000 cubic yards of dirt removed; demolition complete; excavation continues through 2026. Citizen petition to halt project overruled by judge June 18. Targeting world's first zero-carbon convention center — mass timber roof, low-carbon concrete/steel, salvaged materials. LEED Gold. Spring 2029 reopening. Council returns July 23 (18 DAYS).

**July 7 update:** Offices reopen — construction resumes normal pace. $1.6B 'Unconventional ATX' project advancing on schedule: demolition 100% complete, diaphragm wall finished, first footings poured, excavation exceeding 50 ft depth continuing through August. Travis County court ruled June 18 that $1.35B bond financing plan is lawful. Targeting world's first Net Zero Carbon Certified convention center — mass timber roof, low-carbon concrete/steel. Spring 2029 reopening. Council returns July 23 (16 DAYS).

public_comment.instructions: Petition was rejected by court. PAC plans new petition for November ballot. Monitor for new signature-gathering efforts.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: The court ruling clears the way for the convention center project to proceed — a win for pro-development interests. However, Austin United PAC is regrouping for a November ballot push, so this could resurface. For now, this drops to background monitoring. The anti-development coalition (SOS Alliance backing) is worth tracking as it overlaps with NIMBY interests on housing.

issues.title: City Council District 1 — Open Seat Election
issues.summary:
UPDATE (April 21, 2026): The District 1 field has grown to at least 8 candidates. CM Natasha Harper-Madison is term-limited, creating the first open seat in 8 years. Twenty candidates total have filed across all five open council seats (Districts 1, 3, 5, 8, 9).

Declared District 1 candidates include:

• Steven Brown, 41 — Medtronic clinical specialist. Former co-chair of Save Austin Now alongside Matt Mackowiak (Travis County Republican Party chair). Anti-developer framing. Residency concern: homestead exemption outside the district. Has raised ~$6,000.

• Alexandria Anderson, 39 — Retired pro sprinter, MLK Neighborhood Association president. Homeowner in District 1 since 2014. No fundraising reported.

• Naguib 'Simon' Nahas — Housing affordability advocate who has 'worked for years trying to lower rents in Austin.' Supports smaller minimum lot sizes and more housing. Notable: would be the first White council member from District 1 since geographic representation began in 2014 — the seat has historically been held by the city's only Black council member.

• Misael Daguan Ramos, 39 — Ran for the District 1 seat in 2022, giving him name recognition and campaign infrastructure advantage over first-time candidates.

• Kyra Lorena Rogers, 31 — Lives in an apartment off Old Manor Road in District 1. Notably was not yet a registered voter when she filed.

• NEW: Amber Karessa Goodwin, 44 — Assistant district attorney at the Travis County DA's Office. Home address verified in District 1. Brings prosecutorial and justice system experience to the field.

• At least 2 additional candidates have appointed treasurers.

Filing period: July 20 – August 17, 2026.

**May 17 update:** The D1 open seat continues to attract candidates ahead of the July 20-August 17 filing period. At least 8 candidates declared so far. Early campaign finance reports show Steven Brown with ~$6,000 raised — modest by council race standards. The November 3 election will also include Districts 3, 5, 8, and 9. Twenty candidates total have filed across all five open seats. Key evaluation window: watch for candidates' positions on the Density Bonus vote (expected May 28 — 9 DAYS), the BEATF bond proposal, and Project Connect — these three items will define the pro-building litmus test for the next council. ⚡ The Pct 4 runoff early voting continues through May 22, Election Day May 26 — 5 DAYS. This is also a moment to gauge candidate activity and organizing capacity across the broader Austin political landscape.

**May 23 update:** ⚡ MAJOR: Council APPROVED the citywide Density Bonus Program on May 22 — this is a DEFINING litmus test. Developers can now seek 15, 30, 45, or 60 feet of additional height in exchange for affordable housing or community benefits (wider sidewalks, trees). This replaces the controversial DB90 program. Only CM Duchen voted against. Candidates' reactions to the DBC vote will reveal their true positions on growth and density. ⚡ ALSO May 22: Council approved the 2,600-acre 'Dog's Head' annexation in East Austin — a 45-year development deal for one of the largest land parcels in the region, despite significant public opposition and environmental concerns. Both votes define the pro-building trajectory the next council inherits. ⚡ Pct 4 runoff Election Day is May 26 — 3 DAYS AWAY. Early voting ended May 22. No Republican running — winner takes seat.

**May 24 update:** ⚡ Pct 4 runoff Election Day is May 26 — 2 DAYS AWAY. BEATF bond presentation to council May 28 (4 DAYS). New TX AV law takes effect May 28. The next council inherits the DBC framework (approved May 22), Dog's Head implementation, Project Connect's legal battle, and the bond decision. Evaluate candidates' reactions to all of these — they define the pro-building litmus test.

**May 25 update:** ⚡ Pct 4 runoff Election Day is TOMORROW (May 26). BEATF bond presentation to council May 28 (3 DAYS). ⚡ Texas Tribune published a deep-dive (May 21) on Project Connect's scope cuts — the project has shrunk to 10 miles at $8.2B (from the original 26 stops to 15, no longer reaching the airport). The per-mile cost is now 3x the 2020 estimate. Land acquisition costs nearly quadrupled. This reporting adds urgency to how candidates respond to the light rail question. ⚡ AISD budget draft DELAYED to June 4 (was May 22) — Superintendent Segura says they need more time to find ~$50M in additional cuts beyond the $130M already identified. The $181M deficit remains the backdrop for every FY2027 budget conversation. The next council inherits DBC, Dog's Head, Project Connect's legal battle, the bond decision, AND the AISD fiscal crisis.

**May 26 update:** ⚡ Pct 4 runoff is TODAY — results tonight will preview organizing capacity ahead of November. BEATF bond presentation May 28 (2 DAYS). Travis County trial on Project Connect funding legality also May 28 (2 DAYS) — but the TX Supreme Court's procedural ruling requiring resolution of Paxton's jurisdictional plea may delay proceedings. New TX AV law takes effect May 28. AISD budget draft now expected June 4. Filing period for council races opens July 20.

**May 27 update:** ⚡ RUNOFF RESULTS: George Morales won Pct 4 commissioner with ~55% (first new commissioner in 30+ years — infrastructure-focused). Montserrat Garibay won HD-49 state rep with ~60% over former CM Tovo. Both results show pro-establishment Democratic candidates prevailing — a data point for evaluating November council dynamics. ⚡ TOMORROW (May 28) is a MASSIVE council day: (1) BEATF bond presentation — city staff recommended delay to 2028 but CMs Ellis & Laine favor a smaller ~$350M bond focused on parks ($250M), active mobility ($80M), and community facilities ($23M). Decision deadline is mid-August. (2) Downtown Density Bonus hearing (DDB400/DDB850 districts) — extending the density bonus framework into downtown. (3) I-35 Cap & Stitch Program update — resolution to focus funding on two near-term improvements. (4) $156.3M Certificates of Obligation for fire stations, public safety facilities, parks, bridges. (5) Fare-free voting transit resolution for November 2026 election. (6) Housing tax credit support for Decker Lane and Elm Ridge apartments. (7) Rental fee disclosure ordinance. Every one of these items is a litmus test for council candidates. Filing period opens July 20 (54 DAYS).

⚡ MAY 28 UPDATE: Council's packed agenda today is the litmus test for future D1 candidates. BEATF bond presentation ($766.5M vs. $353M parks-only), Downtown DBC hearing, I-35 Cap & Stitch ($104M advance funding approved), $156.3M Certificates of Obligation, housing tax credit support, and rental fee disclosure ordinance all on the agenda. Candidates' positions on these items will define the November race. Filing period opens July 20 (53 DAYS).

**May 30 update:** ⚡ Council voted 6-2-1 on May 29 to proceed with ~$390M bond development (parks, transportation, community facilities) — overruling Mayor Watson's opposition. Housing is NOT in the current bond direction. The bond vote and Watson's loss define the political dynamics candidates must navigate. Also May 29: Council approved oversight guidelines for a new ~$1B natural gas peaker plant — adding energy/environmental policy to the candidate litmus test alongside housing, transit, and public safety. Filing period opens July 20 (51 DAYS).

**May 31 update:** ⚡ Filing period opens July 20 (50 DAYS). Austin Bulldog reports the D1 field continues to grow — seven additional candidates filed treasurers beyond the initial two (Brown, Anderson), making it the most competitive open-seat race in the city. Twenty candidates total have appointed treasurers across all five open seats (Districts 1, 3, 5, 8, 9). The defining litmus tests are set: DBC density bonus (approved May 22), bond vote (6-2-1 to proceed with ~$390M on May 29, housing excluded), gas peaker plant oversight (May 29), and Project Connect's legal limbo. AISD budget proposal expected June 4 (4 DAYS) — 215 FTE educator cuts and larger class sizes will be a campaign issue. Statewide: Vikki Goodwin won the Democratic LG runoff with 68%. James Talarico has an early polling lead over AG Paxton for U.S. Senate — the Paxton-Talarico dynamic could influence the Project Connect legal fight narrative.

**June 1 update:** Filing period opens July 20 (49 DAYS). Seven candidates have appointed treasurers for D1, making it the most competitive open-seat race. Misael Ramos (ran in 2022, placed 2nd with 25.3% / 6,065 votes) has the most campaign infrastructure from a prior run. Goodwin brings prosecutorial experience. Brown's fundraising remains modest (~$6K). The DBC density bonus (approved May 22), bond vote (6-2-1 on May 29, housing excluded, final vote July 23), and Project Connect legal limbo remain the defining litmus tests. AISD budget proposal expected June 4 (3 DAYS) — property sales could generate $45M, narrowing the gap to ~$4M. Board final vote June 18 (17 DAYS). Twenty candidates total across five open seats (D1, D3, D5, D8, D9).

**June 3 update:** Council is now in summer recess — no meetings until July 23. The AISD FY27 budget proposal ($181M deficit) was published June 4 with the Board of Trustees final vote on June 18. 215 educator positions cut. Superintendent Segura is trying to close a ~$50M gap beyond the $130M already identified. Property sales could generate $45M. AISD teachers rallied at the Capitol on June 2 asking legislators to increase school funding — the state funding formula hasn't changed since 2019. The bond final vote is July 23 (50 DAYS). Filing period for council races opens July 20 (47 DAYS). Twenty candidates total across five open seats (D1, D3, D5, D8, D9). The DBC density bonus (approved May 22), bond direction ($390M, housing excluded), gas peaker plant oversight, and AISD fiscal crisis are the defining litmus tests.

AISD teachers rallied at the Capitol on June 2 asking legislators to increase school funding — the state funding formula hasn't changed since 2019. The bond final vote is July 23 (50 DAYS). Filing period for council races opens July 20 (47 DAYS). Twenty candidates total across five open seats (D1, D3, D5, D8, D9). The DBC density bonus (approved May 22), bond direction ($390M, housing excluded), gas peaker plant oversight, and AISD fiscal crisis are the defining litmus tests.

**June 4 update:** Filing period opens July 20 (46 DAYS). Austin Bulldog reports seven candidates have appointed treasurers for D1. AISD budget presented today — education funding likely campaign issue. D1 candidates must position on AISD crisis ($181M deficit, 558 positions, 11 school closures).

George Morales formally appointed Pct 4 commissioner June 11 (3 DAYS). Board final vote on AISD budget June 18 (10 DAYS). Bond final vote July 23 (45 DAYS). **June 9 update:** Filing period opens July 20 (41 DAYS). At least 8 candidates declared for D1, with seven having appointed treasurers — the most competitive open-seat race in the city. **June 13 update:** Filing period opens July 19 (first day is Saturday; first in-person filing day Monday July 20 — 37 DAYS). Filing deadline August 17. issues.title: Commissioner Precinct 4 — George Morales Wins Runoff
issues.summary:
UPDATE (March 3 primary results): George Morales III (40.17%, 9,445 votes) and Susanna Ledesma-Woody (35.18%, 8,273 votes) advance to the May 26 runoff. Ofelia Maldonado Zapata (18.75%) and Gavino Fernandez Jr. (5.9%) were eliminated. Margaret Gómez is retiring after holding this seat since 1995. No Republican is running — the runoff winner takes the seat.

• George Morales III — Former elected Constable Precinct 4, current constable deputy. 20 years with Travis County government, including 16 years as an administrator on Commissioners Court focused on budgeting, housing, and community access.

• Susanna Ledesma-Woody — Project manager at AMD, 14-year Del Valle ISD school board trustee, founding member of Del Valle Community Coalition. Raised in Del Valle, has framed the race as 'policy and governance experience vs. policing experience.' This is her third run for this seat.

**May 17 update:** ⚡ EARLY VOTING STARTS TOMORROW (May 18-22). The runoff is May 26 — just 9 DAYS AWAY. KUT published a comprehensive Travis County voter guide for the May 26 runoff (May 15). Community Impact published detailed candidate Q&As on May 14. The race has crystallized around the contrast between Ledesma-Woody's policy and governance experience (14 years as Del Valle ISD trustee, AMD project manager, founding member of Del Valle Community Coalition) and Morales's policing background (former Precinct 4 Constable). LIV endorsed Ledesma-Woody, criticizing Morales's 'it's my turn' framing. Since no Republican is running, the winner will be the next commissioner — the first new Precinct 4 commissioner in over 30 years. Turnout will be extremely low (8-12%) — individual organizing effort has outsized impact. EARLY VOTING LOCATIONS: Any polling location in Travis County. Vote at votetexas.gov for location finder.

**May 19 update:** ⚡ EARLY VOTING IS NOW OPEN (through May 22). The runoff is May 26 — 6 DAYS AWAY. KUT published a comprehensive profile of both candidates on May 18. Key details: Morales served as Pct 4 Constable for nearly 10 years before stepping down to run; during COVID, his office distributed 380,000 vaccines. Ledesma-Woody has been a Del Valle ISD trustee since 2011 and was the only elected official to vote against the Tesla tax incentive deal. Austin Chronicle published endorsement recommendations for the May 26 runoff. With early voting now underway and turnout expected at 8-12%, every individual vote and organizing effort has outsized impact.

**May 21 update:** ⚡ EARLY VOTING CONTINUES — 2 DAYS OF EARLY VOTING LEFT (through May 22). Election Day is May 26 — 5 DAYS AWAY. This is the highest-leverage local election happening right now. With turnout expected at 8-12%, a small organized canvassing effort can meaningfully shift the outcome. No Republican is running — the runoff winner becomes the next commissioner, the first new Precinct 4 commissioner in over 30 years. LIV endorsed Ledesma-Woody. Vote early today or tomorrow, then spend the remaining days helping others get to the polls.

**May 23 update:** ⚡ EARLY VOTING ENDED May 22. Election Day is May 26 — 3 DAYS AWAY. Last chance to organize. If you haven't voted, vote on Election Day. Spend the next 3 days helping others get to the polls. With 8-12% expected turnout, every door knocked matters. The winner becomes the first new Precinct 4 commissioner in over 30 years — directly influencing county-level land use, transportation infrastructure, and the $5M+ Raising Travis County childcare investment.

**May 24 update:** ⚡ ELECTION DAY IS MAY 26 — 2 DAYS AWAY. If you haven't voted, vote on Election Day. Final organizing window — every door knocked matters in an 8-12% turnout runoff. The winner becomes the first new Precinct 4 commissioner in over 30 years.

The winner — Morales or Ledesma-Woody — becomes the first new Precinct 4 commissioner in over 30 years, directly influencing county-level land use, transportation infrastructure, the $5M+ Raising Travis County childcare investment, and the county's $1.3B+ annual budget. With 8-12% expected turnout, every door knocked today and every ride to the polls tomorrow matters. KUT and Community Impact voter guides are available. No Republican is running — tomorrow's winner takes the seat.

**May 26 update:** ⚡ TODAY IS ELECTION DAY. Polls open 7am-7pm. If you haven't voted, vote TODAY. The winner — Morales or Ledesma-Woody — becomes the first new Precinct 4 commissioner in over 30 years, directly influencing county-level land use, transportation infrastructure, the $5M+ Raising Travis County childcare investment, and the county's $1.3B+ annual budget. With 8-12% expected turnout, every vote matters. Results expected tonight.

**May 27 update:** ⚡ RESULTS: George Morales won the Democratic runoff with approximately 55% of the vote, defeating Susanna Ledesma-Woody (~45%). Morales will be the first new Precinct 4 commissioner in over 30 years — no Republican is running, so he takes the seat and will be sworn in early 2027. Morales identified fixing roads and improving communication between government entities like CapMetro and Central Health as top priorities. He served as Pct 4 Constable for nearly 10 years; during COVID, his office distributed 380,000 vaccines. The commissioners court manages a $2.2 billion budget. SEPARATELY: Montserrat Garibay won the HD-49 state rep runoff with ~60% over former CM Kathie Tovo (~40%) — Garibay, a former teacher and AISD educator, will represent the reliably Democratic district with no Republican opponent. Both she and Tovo opposed the state voucher program and prioritized AISD's $181M deficit.

**May 29 update:** ⚡ RESULT: George Morales won the May 26 runoff ~55% to ~45% over Ledesma-Woody — first new Pct 4 commissioner in 30+ years. Morales brings infrastructure-focused priorities and a law enforcement background (former constable). He will influence county-level childcare funding ($17.65M+ Raising Travis County), public safety coordination, and infrastructure investment. HD-49: Montserrat Garibay won ~60% over former CM Kathie Tovo, running pro-education and anti-voucher.

issues.title: Homelessness Strategic Plan Adopted — 650 New Shelter Beds Planned
issues.summary:
⚡ UPDATE (May 7, 2026): HSO Director David Gray presented the expanded encampment sweep plan to Council at the MAY 5 WORK SESSION. Council received briefings on both the sweeps expansion and operational modifications within Homeless Strategies and Operations. Council also discussed a resolution directing the City Manager to postpone departmental reorganizations associated with the 'One ATS' initiative until council is presented with sufficient information.

May 7: Council APPROVED $497,299 in one-time funding for Integral Care's Homeless Health and Wellness Center program — an amendment to the existing interlocal agreement expanding crisis health services for people experiencing homelessness.

BACKGROUND: Austin City Council approved the AHSO's first-ever strategic plan (2025-2027) on March 26. Key elements: 650 new shelter beds, 2 new housing navigation centers, $101M in proposed additional funding. Director David Gray noted ~6,500 people are currently living on the streets — 'one shelter bed for every five people sleeping unhoused.' Endeavors has taken over operation of downtown shelters (ARCH, Eighth Street) under emergency contract through September 2026.

April 13: AHSO launched the operator solicitation for the South Austin Housing Navigation Center — the city's FIRST city-owned navigation center. Up to $250,000 in funding is available. The center is expected to open late summer or early fall 2026.

⚡ SWEEPS EXPANSION: The plan details 42 city staff across six full-time Homeless Encampment Management (HEM) teams — three geographic teams (North, South, Central Austin) plus teams for transportation corridors, waterways, and litter cleanup. Each team includes two APD officers. CRITICAL CHANGE: outreach workers will NO LONGER be embedded in sweep teams. VOCAL-TX and other advocates are pushing back hard. A series of sweeps last fall cost over $800,000 without leading many people out of homelessness. City leaders acknowledge there aren't enough shelters for everyone displaced.

The $101M funding ask will compete with other priorities in FY2027 budget hearings. The $766.5M BEATF bond proposal (May 6) includes public facilities funding that could support a purpose-built shelter to replace the Marshalling Yard.

**May 17 update:** HSO briefed council on operational modifications. Encampment management operations have expanded from three to five days per week as of mid-May, using a six-step process (prioritization, outreach, 72-hour notice, closure). The city now has 400 more shelter beds than two years ago and has added over 1,100 permanent supportive housing units in the past three years, though only 45 shelter beds are currently open. Austin's local housing voucher program supporting 380+ chronically homeless residents faces cuts amid the $26.4M citywide budget deficit. The South Austin Housing Navigation Center operator solicitation (April 13) closes soon — this will be the city's FIRST city-owned navigation center, expected to open late summer/early fall 2026. The expanded sweeps are now operational five days per week — monitor for early data on outcomes and community impact.

**May 23 update:** Council approved the Density Bonus Program on May 22, expanding housing production tools. The 2,600-acre Dog's Head annexation was also approved — both moves increase the housing pipeline that feeds into the HSO's permanent housing exit strategy. The BEATF bond proposal ($766.5M or $353M parks-only alternative) goes to council May 28. For homelessness services, the full $766.5M package includes $131.1M in public facilities funding that could support shelter infrastructure.

**May 24 update:** BEATF bond presentation May 28 (4 DAYS). The full $766.5M package includes $131.1M for public facilities that could fund shelter infrastructure — the $353M parks-only counter-proposal excludes this. AISD board final vote on budget with 215 FTE educator cuts is June 18 (25 DAYS).

The board must pass a budget by end of June. The delay deepens uncertainty for families and educators already facing 215 FTE cuts. Encampment management teams continue operating five days/week citywide. The South Austin Housing Navigation Center operator solicitation remains pending — this would be the city's FIRST city-owned navigation center.

**May 26 update:** BEATF bond presentation May 28 (2 DAYS) — the full $766.5M package includes $131.1M for public facilities (shelter infrastructure). Pct 4 runoff is TODAY. AISD budget draft delayed to June 4. Encampment management teams continue operating five days/week citywide.

George Morales won the Pct 4 runoff with ~55%. AISD budget draft delayed to June 4. Encampment management teams continue operating five days/week citywide.

**May 30 update:** ⚡ Council voted 6-2-1 on May 29 to proceed with ~$390M bond development focused on parks, transportation, and community facilities. The $131.1M public facilities allocation (which could fund shelter infrastructure) is NOT in the current $390M direction. The BEATF's full $766.5M package appears dead. For homelessness services, the bond exclusion means the $101M HSO funding ask and shelter infrastructure investments will depend entirely on operating budget allocations — which face $16.8M in social services cuts and a $26.4M FY2027 deficit. AISD budget draft expected June 4 (5 DAYS). Encampment management teams continue operating five days/week.

issues.title: HOME Initiative Delivering Results — Austin Rents Down 16%, Steepest Drop in US
issues.summary:
UPDATE (March 2026): A Pew Charitable Trusts report (March 18, 2026) confirms that Austin's housing construction surge — driven by the HOME Initiative and other pro-building policies — produced the steepest rent decline of any large U.S. city from 2021 to 2026. Key numbers:

• From 2015-2024, Austin added 120,000 units to its housing stock — a 30% increase, more than 3x the national rate (9%).
• Austin's median rent fell from $1,546 (Dec 2021) to $1,296 (Jan 2026) — a 16% decline.
• Austin's median rent is now 4% BELOW the U.S. median ($1,353) after being 15% above it in 2021.
• The combination of strong demand and proactive policy changes (HOME Phase 1 adopted Dec 2023, Phase 2 adopted May 2024) spurred the supply surge.

HOME Initiative implementation milestones: Phase 1 applications accepted Feb 2024, Phase 2 applications accepted Aug-Nov 2024. The initiative now permits up to 3 units on many single-family lots and reduced minimum lot sizes to 1,800 sq ft. Compatibility standards were also reformed — enforcement zones reduced from 540 feet to 75 feet, allowing more height flexibility near single-family homes.

This is concrete, measurable evidence that building more housing lowers costs. Austin is now the national proof point for YIMBY policy.

**June 22 update:** Development rules overhaul implementation continues — minimum lot sizes reduced to 1,800 sq ft, DBC in effect, missing middle zoning districts created. Bond survey closes TOMORROW — housing/homelessness ranked second priority (18.5%) among 2,002 respondents, reinforcing public demand for continued housing investment. Council on recess until July 23 (31 DAYS).

**June 23 update:** Bond survey CLOSED TODAY — housing/homelessness ranked second priority (18.5%) among 2,000+ respondents, reinforcing public demand for continued housing investment despite exclusion from ~$390M bond direction. Council on recess until July 23 (30 DAYS).

**June 27 update:** DBC: zero applications in 27 days since launch — SB 840 by-right multifamily reduces developer incentive for city programs. Missing middle draft ordinances due March 2027. Council on recess until July 23 (26 DAYS).

**June 28 update:** DBC: zero applications in 28 days since launch — SB 840 by-right multifamily reduces developer incentive for city density bonus programs. Missing middle draft ordinances due March 2027. Council on recess until July 23 (25 DAYS).

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: This is the scoreboard. Austin's 16% rent decline — the steepest in any large US city — is direct evidence that the YIMBY policy agenda works. Building more housing lowers costs. This data is your most powerful argument in every future rezoning fight, council race, and public hearing. The Pew report gives this national credibility. Share it. Cite it. Use it to counter every 'building doesn't help' argument you'll hear in the golf course rezoning and council election debates.

issues.title: Missing Middle & Mixed-Use Zoning Expansion — Advancing Through Council
issues.summary:
UPDATE (April 26, 2026): Austin City Council advanced the expansion of missing middle residential and mixed-use zoning on March 26, building on the HOME Initiative. The proposal creates two new zoning categories — one for multiunit 'missing middle' housing (townhomes, cottage courts, single-stair buildings) and one for neighborhood-scale mixed-use (retail on ground floor, housing above).

A January 2026 city zoning study recommended allowing 4-6 units on lots of at least 5,750 square feet. Over the past 40 years, less than 1% of housing built in Austin has been 'missing middle' — the city's 1984 land development code largely supports only single-family homes or large apartment complexes.

⚡ DBC UPDATE (May 8): Council received a WORK SESSION BRIEFING on the citywide Density Bonus program (DBC) on May 5. The DBC has five tiers adding 0-60 feet of extra height in multifamily and commercial zones in exchange for affordable housing. Central affordability proposal: 10% of units affordable at each tier — ownership at 80% MFI, rental at 50% MFI. The DBC introduces two new 'combining districts' offering developers height bonuses of roughly 400 feet or 850 feet above base entitlements — maximum heights up to 750 feet under the lower tier and 1,200 feet under the higher one, a dramatic jump from the current 350-foot cap. Developers face stricter parking caps and must meet design standards (7 for smaller bonus, 10 for larger) while providing on-site affordable housing or paying fees in lieu. Planning Commission hearing HELD April 28; ⚡ COUNCIL VOTE EXPECTED MAY 28 (9 DAYS). This responds to SB 840 (state 'residential in commercial' law) and replaces the controversial prior density bonus program.

Additionally, on April 9, Austin officials approved more than $50 million for local affordable housing initiatives, including $29.27 million for existing and planned developments. On April 12, City Council approved a new development near West Campus. And on March 26, Council approved the Montopolis Saxon Lane property transfer (0.09-acre city-owned site) for affordable housing development — enabled by HOME Initiative lot size reductions.

Staff will spend the next year developing missing middle draft ordinances, with completion expected by March 2027.

**May 17 update:** Council received a detailed work session briefing on the Citywide Density Bonus Program on May 5. Staff proposed five new bonus tiers ranging from no additional height to up to 60 additional feet beyond base zoning, responding to SB 840 requirements. SB 840 ('residential in commercial') allows unlimited FAR and up to 54 units/acre by right in commercial zones — undermining the city's traditional density bonus leverage for affordable housing. More than 20% of Austin's income-restricted housing was created through density bonus programs now affected by SB 840. The council vote on the revised DBC is expected May 28 (9 DAYS). Meanwhile, Council voted 9-1 on May 8 to push the HOME Initiative deeper into single-family neighborhoods — loosening lot-size, setback, and subdivision rules for missing middle housing. Over 600 HOME applications have been approved, adding 1,200+ new housing units. CM Duchen cast the lone no vote, citing concerns about luxury housing displacing lower-cost existing homes. Community Not Commodity published a detailed critique (May 11) arguing the DBC proposal would allow 90-foot buildings 25 feet from single-family lot lines and 120-foot buildings 50 feet from lot lines — expect organized opposition at the May 28 council vote.

**May 19 update:** The DBC council vote date has been clarified: Austin City Council has a regular meeting on May 21 where the DBC may be considered, though the full council vote is still expected May 28 (9 DAYS). Community Not Commodity's detailed opposition piece (May 11) continues to circulate — expect organized opposition at the council vote.

**May 20 update:** ⚡ THE DBC COUNCIL VOTE IS TOMORROW (MAY 21). Austin City Council's regular meeting agenda for May 21 includes a PUBLIC HEARING AND CONSIDERATION of an ordinance creating the new citywide density bonus zoning districts, replacing DB90 and VMU. This is the vote — not May 28 as previously expected. The ordinance would amend City Code Title 25 to create new zoning districts allowing additional height (including more than 30 feet above base) in exchange for affordable housing and community benefits, applicable to commercial and multifamily base zones citywide. Community Not Commodity's opposition campaign continues to circulate. Show up or submit written testimony TODAY.

**May 21 update:** ⚡ DBC PUBLIC HEARING IS TODAY. Austin City Council's regular meeting agenda confirms the public hearing and first reading of the citywide Density Bonus Combining Districts ordinance. This is the most significant zoning reform since the HOME Initiative — replacing the legacy DB90 and VMU programs with a new framework that responds to SB 840. Community Not Commodity organized opposition is expected at the hearing. A majority of council members have signaled support in prior work sessions. If approved on first reading today, final passage would follow at a subsequent meeting. The DBC, combined with the Light Rail Transit Overlay adopted May 16 and the HOME Initiative expansions, completes a comprehensive transit-oriented development framework for Austin. Monitor council proceedings for the vote outcome.

**May 24 update:** ⚡ DBC IS NOW LAW. Council APPROVED the citywide Density Bonus Combining Districts on May 22 (only Duchen opposed). This completes the transit-oriented development trifecta: HOME Initiative + Light Rail Transit Overlay (May 16) + DBC (May 22). Austin now has the most comprehensive pro-housing zoning framework of any major Texas city. The DBC replaces the legacy DB90 and VMU programs. Missing middle draft ordinances remain in development — staff completion expected by March 2027.

**June 22 update:** DBC in effect since May 22; missing middle draft ordinances in development (due March 2027). Council on summer recess until July 23 (31 DAYS). Bond survey closes TOMORROW — transportation and housing top public priorities. AISD boundary workshops TODAY reshaping school attendance zones, which intersect with neighborhood-level housing demand.

**June 23 update:** Bond survey CLOSED TODAY — transportation and housing/homelessness tied as top priorities. AISD virtual boundary workshop TONIGHT (6-7:30 PM). Missing middle draft ordinances due March 2027. Council on recess until July 23 (30 DAYS).

**June 28 update:** Missing middle draft ordinances due March 2027. Development rules overhaul in implementation phase — minimum lot size reduced from 5,750 to 1,800 sq ft citywide (most significant change in 80 years). Council on recess until July 23 (25 DAYS).

public_comment.instructions: Community engagement ongoing through 2026-2027. Monitor austintexas.gov/development-services/home-amendments for public hearing schedules.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: This is HOME Initiative Phase 3 in everything but name. The missing middle zoning categories create purpose-built pathways for the gentle density that makes neighborhoods walkable — townhomes, cottage courts, small mixed-use buildings. This is especially relevant for 78702 where transit-oriented development along the future light rail corridor needs exactly this kind of zoning. ⚡ THE DBC PUBLIC HEARING IS TODAY (May 21) — the public hearing is underway. Show up at council or submit written testimony. Combined with the Light Rail Transit Overlay (adopted May 16), a DBC approval today locks in Austin's comprehensive transit-oriented development framework.

issues.title: 2026 Bond Package — Council Votes 6-2-1 to Proceed, ~$390M Parks-Focused
issues.summary:
⚡ MAJOR UPDATE (May 6, 2026): The Bond Election Advisory Task Force (BEATF) has released its formal proposals — TWO OPTIONS for council consideration:

⚡ PRIMARY PROPOSAL: $766.53 MILLION (passed UNANIMOUSLY by the task force):
• Affordable housing: $200M
• Parks and recreation: $175M
• Transportation (sidewalks, Vision Zero, trails): $147M
• Public facilities (community centers, animal shelter, homeless services): $131.1M
• Drainage and flood prevention: $113.43M

⚡ ALTERNATIVE PROPOSAL: ~$440 MILLION (passed with abstentions and opposing votes):
• Parks, mobility, and public facilities only
• EXCLUDES housing and watershed protection

TAX IMPACT: The $766.5M package would increase median homeowner property taxes by approximately $100/year. The smaller option would cost roughly $60/year.

Task force member Tina Cannon opposed the council-requested smaller option, stating the city faces 'over $10 billion in unmet needs.' Members emphasized East Austin priorities, particularly the George Washington Carver Museum and Colony Park library improvements.

Some officials suggested pursuing the smaller package in 2026 and a larger bond in 2028 when debt capacity improves. City budget staff had recommended a cap of $687M based on debt capacity.

BUDGET CONTEXT (April 22): FY2027 budget talks have begun in earnest. The general fund is expected to grow only ~3%, with no pay raises planned for civilian city employees. Some council members said an early priority is maintaining the nearly $9M in affordable housing funding set to be eliminated. Budget work sessions set to begin in July with adoption expected in August.

⚡ TIMELINE: BEATF will present its formal recommendation to City Council on MAY 28 — 9 DAYS AWAY. Council will then define the final bond package and call an election in August. The fiscal pressure is acute — voters rejected Proposition Q last November, which would have generated $110M for parks and homelessness services. That rejection, combined with the FY2027 $26.4M projected deficit, means the bond package is the primary remaining mechanism for large-scale capital investment.

**May 17 update:** BEATF finalized two proposals: the full $766.53M package and a narrower $436M alternative (excluding housing). The full package exceeds the $700M cap recommended by city finance staff. Late BEATF discussions (May 12-14) focused on East Austin priorities — members agreed to reduce some park, housing, and transportation allocations to fully fund the George Washington Carver Museum and Colony Park library/health center. The task force also recommended a 'contract with the voters' requiring clear project timelines and more participation from other local governments in future bond processes. issues.title: Waymo AV Blocks Ambulance — Committee Held Meeting Without Waymo
issues.summary:
UPDATE (April 26, 2026): A Waymo autonomous vehicle blocked an ambulance responding to the mass shooting at Buford's on West 6th Street on March 1. Video showed the driverless car stopped across both lanes; an APD officer had to manually move it. ATCEMS Chief Luckritz said the incident didn't affect overall response but it was 'unacceptable' that personnel had to divert attention during an active emergency. Five council members (Qadri, Vela, Velasquez, Ellis, Laine) signed a letter inviting Waymo to a joint Public Safety and Mobility Committee meeting on April 29 to discuss coordination with emergency services.

⚡ APRIL 25: WAYMO DECLINED the council invitation, saying it had 'already had substantive conversations directly with all five requesting Council offices, more than 15 city officials, and the highest levels of state government.'

⚡ APRIL 29 — MEETING HELD WITHOUT WAYMO: The joint Public Safety and Mobility Committee proceeded as scheduled (11am-1pm) without Waymo representation. Council members heard testimony from ATCEMS, APD, and members of the public regarding autonomous vehicle safety coordination with emergency services. CM Qadri reiterated: 'Their absence speaks for itself.' Committee members discussed potential regulatory options including requiring AV operators to coordinate directly with emergency dispatch and establishing emergency vehicle right-of-way protocols for autonomous vehicles. NEXT STEPS: Council staff directed to research municipal regulatory authority over AV operations and report back at a future council session. Staff has recommended a 1,000-FOOT AV EXCLUSION ZONE around active emergency scenes. Additionally, the city is pushing for new state driverless vehicle legislation — new state DMV enforcement authority takes effect May 28.

CONTEXT: Council also passed the TRUST Act on April 24, establishing a new regulatory framework for surveillance technology used by city departments. The ordinance requires council approval before deploying new data-collection technology, bans facial recognition, and limits how collected data can be used. The TRUST Act reflects a broader council posture toward technology accountability — the same impulse driving the Waymo hearing.

**May 17 update:** Waymo issued a national recall of 3,791 robotaxis after a vehicle was swept away during San Antonio flash flooding on April 20. The over-the-air software fix addresses the vehicle's failure to stop on higher-speed roadways when detecting potentially flooded lanes. ⚡ KEY DATE: A new Texas law takes effect May 28 (7 DAYS) requiring AV companies to obtain Texas DMV authorization before operating commercially; the DMV can now restrict operators whose vehicles cause serious bodily injury. Austin has two representatives on the new Automated Vehicle Regulation Advisory Committee. Waymo's Austin fleet has grown to approximately 300 vehicles. Council staff's recommended 1,000-foot AV exclusion zone around active emergency scenes is still pending review.

⚡ MAY 28 UPDATE: NEW TX LAW TAKES EFFECT TODAY. Senate Bill 2807 now requires all companies operating automated vehicles commercially to obtain authorization through the Texas DMV Motor Carrier Credentialing System. Waymo (with ~300 robotaxis in Austin) is among 10 companies that have submitted plans to Texas DPS. As of late April, only 6 of 10 companies had been authorized. The new law requires vehicles to comply with all TX traffic laws, be equipped with recording devices, and be capable of achieving a 'minimal risk condition' if the automated driving system fails.

**June 4 update:** SB 2807 now fully in effect since May 28. All four AV companies operating in Austin (Waymo, Zoox, AV-Ride, Tesla) are state-authorized through TxDMV. City AV dashboard shows 75 incidents in 2026 including 1 collision, 8 near misses, and 7 incidents of ignoring police direction.

**June 22 update:** Council on summer recess until July 23 (31 DAYS). SB 2807 in effect since May 28 — all four AV companies in Austin (Waymo, Zoox, AV-Ride, Tesla) are state-authorized. Monitor for staff report on municipal AV regulatory authority when council returns.

**June 28 update:** Council on recess until July 23 (25 DAYS). SB 2807 in effect since May 28 — all four AV companies in Austin (Waymo, Zoox, AV-Ride, Tesla) are state-authorized. Monitor for staff report on municipal AV regulatory authority when council returns.

public_comment.instructions: Joint Public Safety and Mobility Committee meeting held April 29 without Waymo. Monitor council for follow-up regulatory actions.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: The April 29 meeting proceeded without Waymo — confirming the company's refusal to engage directly with elected officials on a public safety matter. Council members are now exploring regulatory options, including emergency vehicle right-of-way protocols for AVs and mandatory coordination with dispatch. Combined with the TRUST Act (April 24), Austin is establishing a clear framework: technology companies operating in public spaces must be accountable to the communities they serve. Watch for the staff report on municipal regulatory authority — this could set precedent for how cities nationwide handle AV safety oversight.

issues.title: Project Connect — TX Supreme Court Still Pending, $230M Land Acquisition Approved
issues.governing_body: Austin Transit Partnership / Texas Supreme Court
issues.summary:
UPDATE (May 8, 2026): The Texas Supreme Court has STILL NOT ruled on AG Paxton's challenge to Project Connect's funding mechanism — now 99 DAYS since oral arguments on February 10. The procedural question remains: whether the AG's office was allowed to file an early appeal. Meanwhile, the project continues advancing:

• January 2026: Federal environmental review (Record of Decision) signed off by Trump administration — a critical milestone for ~$4 billion in FTA Capital Investment Grant funding.
• February 18, 2026: Austin Transit Partnership approved a $60 million design-build contract with Austin Rail Constructors for Phase 1.
⚡ APRIL 15: ATP approved a $25 MILLION contract with Kiewit Austin Partnership for the Phase 1 operations and maintenance facility. ATP also approved spending up to $230 MILLION to acquire up to 18 parcels for the rail corridor and maintenance facility — the first major land acquisition push. Mayor Watson noted this is 'the beginning of the process where people may anticipate condemnation actions and eminent domain actions.' ATP will negotiate initially; the city handles eminent domain if needed (12-18 month process).
• Early field work continues along the corridor. Full construction expected to begin 2027. Target completion: 2033.

APRIL 13: Mayor Watson blocked ATP's proposed $47 million downtown office deal ($32M lease + $15M buildout for 51,600 sq ft at Congress & Cesar Chavez), calling it 'inappropriate' given the city's budget challenges. Watson pushed ATP to co-locate with CapMetro. The vote was postponed indefinitely — signals political pressure on ATP to demonstrate spending restraint while the legal cloud persists.

The legal uncertainty remains the single biggest risk: if the court sides with Paxton, the entire $8.23 billion project — and its $166M/year dedicated tax revenue — could be jeopardized. But the continued advancement of contracts, land acquisition, and federal milestones signals confidence from ATP leadership.

As of May 19, the court has gone 99 DAYS since oral arguments with no ruling — an unusually long wait suggesting the justices may be divided or drafting a complex opinion.

issues.title: COTA $985M Resort Project — Council Approves Incentive Deal
issues.summary:
UPDATE (April 26, 2026): Austin City Council APPROVED the incentive deal on April 23 for a $985 million hotel and convention center project at Circuit of the Americas. Houston-based RIDA Development Corporation will build a 1,000-room hotel and 170,000-square-foot conference center adjacent to the Formula 1 track. The deal is expected to create about 900 permanent full-time hospitality, food service, maintenance, and management jobs, plus ~4,000 temporary construction jobs.

The incentive structure is performance-based: Austin will rebate a portion of hotel occupancy tax revenue (capped at 8.25% of gross room night revenue) over up to 30 years, with payments not beginning until the hotel is operational and generating tax revenue. Officials estimate the project could generate roughly $200 million in property and sales tax revenue over three decades. The deal breaks down roughly 89% private / 11% public investment — city staff noted 'anything 15% or below is considered a good use of public dollars.' Council also advanced a zoning update on April 23, with final PUD approval expected late May.

TIMELINE: RIDA anticipates an 18-month entitlement process followed by 30-36 months of construction. The resort will include dining, a golf course, driving range, and a lazy river similar to RIDA's Marriott Marquis Houston.

RIDA reached a labor peace agreement with Unite Here Local 23. Council also approved a land title transfer required under state law for the project to qualify for state hotel occupancy and sales tax rebates.

The resort is designed to serve mid-sized events and complement — not compete with — Austin's new downtown convention center currently under construction ($1.6B, opening late 2028).

**June 22 update:** Convention center D-wall construction underway; Judge Thomas ruled June 18 that $1.35B bond funding is lawful — reinforcing the fiscal framework for both the downtown convention center and COTA resort. RIDA's 18-month entitlement process continues. Council on recess until July 23 (31 DAYS).

**June 28 update:** RIDA's 18-month entitlement process continues. Convention center D-wall construction complete; structural steel positioning underway. Council on recess until July 23 (25 DAYS).

public_comment.instructions: Deal approved April 23. Monitor implementation and hotel construction timeline.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: Deal done. A $985M private investment with only 11% public exposure and 900 permanent jobs is a favorable outcome by city staff metrics. The performance-based structure (no payments until the hotel is generating revenue) mitigates risk. $200M in projected property/sales tax revenue over 30 years strengthens Austin's fiscal position — relevant context as the city navigates a $26.4M FY2027 deficit. This doesn't directly advance housing supply, but fiscal health supports everything else.

issues.title: AISD $181M Budget Deficit — Budget Meetings Continue Through June
issues.summary:
⚡ UPDATE (May 8, 2026): TODAY — campuses begin receiving staffing allocations (May 8-11). Leadership meets with potentially affected staff May 12-15.

⚡ MAY 6 BOMBSHELL (KUT): AISD proposes LARGER CLASS SIZES and CUTS TO TEACHER PLANNING TIME at most campuses. The preliminary budget spares Band 1 campuses (all TAP campuses and the most vulnerable schools per the Support and Resource Index), but ALL OTHER CAMPUSES face increased class sizes and reduced planning periods. Campus-level changes will phase in over the next two school years.

⚡ CUTS IDENTIFIED:
• Central & department budgets: $73.8M in reductions, including eliminating the Deputy Superintendent of Business and Operations vacancy and other central office positions
• Campus budgets: $33.9M in proposed cuts — now confirmed to include larger class sizes
• Revenue generation: $45M from real estate monetization
• 10 campus closures: Expected to save $21M, with $17M reinvested into school improvements

⚡ POSITIONS PROTECTED:
• Librarians and counselors will NOT be eliminated
• Teacher planning time and class sizes PROTECTED ONLY at turnaround/unacceptable-rated campuses (Band 1)
• Bilingual and special education stipends prioritized

⚡ CRITICAL TIMELINE:
• ⚡ May 8-11: Campuses receive staffing allocations (HAPPENING NOW)
• May 9: Virtual budget meeting, 11am-noon
• May 12-15: Leadership meets with potentially affected staff
• May 21: Recommended Budget presented to Board
• June 18: Final board vote on budget
• By June 19: Notifications sent to impacted employees

BACKGROUND: The district faces a $181M FY2027 deficit. Current-year deficit: $49M. The 10 campus closures already approved for 2026-27 will proceed, but Superintendent Segura paused additional closures (April 24). Primary drivers: declining enrollment (3,000+ students lost — each worth $14,889 in per-pupil funding), Austin property values down 18% from 2022 peak, and Texas 'Robin Hood' school finance recapture.

**May 17 update:** AISD confirmed 215 full-time educator position cuts — 85 elementary, 51 middle school, 79 high school — for 2026-27. Staffing allocations went to campuses May 8-11; leadership met with potentially affected staff May 12-15. Central and department reductions total $73.8M. ⚡ NEXT: The recommended budget is presented to the Board on May 21 — 4 DAYS AWAY. Final Board of Trustees vote: June 18. The district has lost nearly 3,200 students from last school year, compounding the deficit alongside decreased property values and a delayed $26M property sale. The larger class sizes and cuts to teacher planning time (May 6 KUT report) are now being implemented at all non-Band 1 campuses. Superintendent Segura clarified that not all 215 positions will result in layoffs — hundreds of vacancies exist — but teacher-student ratios will increase at most campuses.

**May 19 update:** ⚡ The recommended budget presentation to the Board is TOMORROW (May 21). KVUE reported under the headline 'There are no good options' on the preliminary budget outlook. Austin Chronicle reported AISD proposes cutting over 200 full-time positions. The 215 confirmed educator position cuts (85 elementary, 51 middle school, 79 high school) and larger class sizes at non-Band 1 campuses remain the core of the budget plan. Final Board vote: June 18.

**May 21 update:** ⚡ THE RECOMMENDED BUDGET IS BEING PRESENTED TO THE BOARD TODAY. Superintendent Segura is presenting the FY2027 recommended budget addressing the $181M deficit. The 215 confirmed educator position cuts, larger class sizes at non-Band 1 campuses, and $73.8M in central/department reductions form the core of the plan. The district has lost nearly 3,200 students from last school year, compounding the deficit. The board will discuss and take public input today, with the final vote scheduled for June 18 — 28 DAYS AWAY. The budget's impact on family retention in urban Austin connects directly to the housing and family formation agendas tracked by Austin Abundance Project and Austin YIMBY Action.

⚡ MAY 28 UPDATE: Budget draft remains delayed — AISD pushed the recommended budget presentation from May 22 to June 4 (7 DAYS). Superintendent Segura has identified $152.7M in potential cuts ($73.8M central office, $33.9M campus budgets, $45M real estate revenue) against the $181M deficit. Board final vote June 18 (21 DAYS). KUT reports the district 'still hasn't released a plan' despite needing to pass a budget by end of June. BEATF's bond package includes no AISD funding — school finance is a separate track. Families navigating city bond, county budget, AND school district budget pressures simultaneously.

**May 29 update:** AISD budget draft still delayed — expected June 4 (6 DAYS). Superintendent Segura has identified $152.7M in potential cuts but needs ~$50M more to close the $181M deficit. 215 FTE educator cuts confirmed (85 elementary, 51 middle, 79 high school). Board final vote June 18 (20 DAYS).

**May 30 update:** AISD budget draft expected June 4 (5 DAYS). Board final vote June 18 (19 DAYS). The city's May 29 bond vote to proceed with ~$390M parks-focused bond does NOT include AISD funding — school finance remains a separate track. Families face simultaneous fiscal pressure from city bond, county budget, and school district cuts. The ~$50M gap between identified cuts ($152.7M) and the full $181M deficit remains unresolved.

**May 31 update:** ⚡ AISD will split librarians across some campuses — previously these positions were said to be protected. Budget proposal expected June 4 (4 DAYS). Board final vote June 18 (18 DAYS). The $181M deficit remains unresolved — identified cuts total $152.7M, leaving a ~$28M gap. 215 FTE educator positions cut. Superintendent Segura needs board approval by end of June. The budget's impact on family retention in urban Austin connects directly to housing and enrollment — each student lost costs the district ~$14,889 in per-pupil funding.

**June 1 update:** Superintendent's budget proposal expected June 4 (3 DAYS). Officials now say they hope to generate $45M by selling district-owned properties, narrowing the remaining shortfall to roughly $4M. Proposal includes cutting districtwide magnet transportation, reducing police officers, changing substitute pay, cutting 215 FTE educator positions (85 elementary, 51 middle, 79 high school), reducing employee duty days, and adding instructional days. Board final vote June 18 (17 DAYS). Budget takes effect July 1.

**June 3 update:** AISD FY27 budget proposal ($181M deficit) was published June 4 with the Board of Trustees final vote scheduled for June 18. 215 full-time educator positions are being cut (85 elementary, 51 middle, 79 high school), though some may be filled through attrition of open vacancies. Superintendent Segura has identified $130M in cuts but is still trying to close a ~$50M gap. The district is exploring selling surplus properties which could generate ~$45M. Class sizes will increase for grades 2-5 and planning time will be cut for secondary teachers. AISD teachers rallied at the Texas Capitol on June 2, asking legislators to increase school funding. The state funding formula — the Basic Allotment — has not been adjusted since 2019, meaning districts absorb inflation without additional state support. This is a statewide structural problem, not just an AISD management issue. The fiscal crisis creates both headwinds and tailwinds for housing policy: budget pressure incentivizes the city to approve new development (property tax revenue), but school quality concerns can fuel NIMBY arguments against density. Council candidates must articulate a position on school funding alongside housing and transit.

**June 4 update:** AISD FY27 BUDGET PROPOSAL PRESENTED TODAY to Board of Trustees. Superintendent Segura's plan affects 558 positions (teachers, librarians, counselors, central staff, police). Key measures: 11 schools closing this summer ($21M savings), $60M from selling/monetizing 4 AISD properties, $31M campus changes (larger class sizes, reduced planning periods, librarian cuts), $17M central office cuts, $2.4M transportation changes (hub model at elementary schools, late-night activity buses discontinued). Certified teachers guaranteed a job; non-certified staff may lose positions. Board final vote June 18 (14 DAYS).

**June 5 update:** Board of Trustees final vote on FY27 budget is June 18 — 13 DAYS AWAY. The $181M deficit plan affecting 558 positions remains the defining local education issue heading into November council races.

**June 7 update:** Parents and teachers rallied on June 5 to protest the budget cuts — the protest drew significant media coverage and community attention. The recommended budget affects 558 positions total across the district (teachers, librarians, counselors, police, and central staff). 11 schools closing this summer. issues.title: Austin Backs Down on ICE Policy — APD Updating General Orders to Cooperate
issues.summary:
UPDATE (April 26, 2026): Austin is backing down. On April 24, APD announced it will UPDATE ITS GENERAL ORDERS regarding cooperation with ICE and immigration operations, following Governor Abbott's threat to pull $2.5 million in state public safety grants. The Governor's Public Safety Office granted Austin an extension after what the city described as 'good faith discussions.' Abbott expects any revised policy to require 'full cooperation with DHS, including notifying DHS and detaining individuals subject to removal proceedings.'

This follows the same pattern as Houston (changed its ordinance April 22) and Dallas (revised its ICE policy April 23-24). All three major Texas cities have now yielded to the state's fiscal pressure.

BACKGROUND: The dispute centered on Austin City Council's March 4 revisions to policies governing how APD officers work with Immigration and Customs Enforcement. Under the revised policy, officers were NOT authorized to make arrests or detentions based solely on ICE administrative warrants. The Governor's Public Safety Office demanded repeal or face termination of all public safety grants.

The $2.5M at stake funds public safety programs. In a city facing a $26.4M FY2027 deficit with $16.8M in proposed social services cuts, the fiscal pressure proved decisive. Unlike the 2017 'sanctuary city' fight (when Austin absorbed the consequences), the 2026 fiscal environment left no room to hold the line.

**June 22 update:** Council on summer recess until July 23 (31 DAYS). APD general orders update on ICE cooperation remains in effect. Filing period for council races opens July 19 (27 DAYS) — candidates' positions on immigration enforcement and state-vs-local authority will be campaign issues.

**June 27 update:** Council on recess until July 23 (26 DAYS). City Manager budget presentation July 16 (19 DAYS) — federal funding impacts on city services remain a budget consideration.

**June 28 update:** Council on recess until July 23 (25 DAYS). City Manager budget presentation July 16 (18 DAYS) — federal funding impacts on city services remain a budget consideration.

public_comment.instructions: Monitor council response to the deadline. The next council meeting will likely address this issue.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: Austin joined Houston and Dallas in yielding to Abbott's fiscal pressure. The $2.5M grant preservation is pragmatically defensible — with 340 APD vacancies and a $26.4M deficit, losing additional public safety funding was untenable. But the pattern matters: all three major Texas cities backed down within a week, demonstrating that state-level fiscal leverage can override local policy preferences. This is relevant context as five council seats turn over in November — candidates' positions on state-vs-local authority and immigrant community trust will matter. The policy reversal may also affect AISD enrollment trends, since immigration enforcement fears were identified as a driver of the 3,000+ student decline.

issues.title: TRUST Act Passed — Austin Regulates Surveillance Technology
issues.summary:
NEW (April 24, 2026): Austin City Council passed the Transparent and Responsible Use of Surveillance Technology Act (TRUST Act), establishing a new regulatory framework for how city departments use surveillance technology.

Key provisions:
• City departments must obtain council approval before deploying new technology that collects data on residents
• Departments must disclose how data is collected, stored, and shared
• Facial recognition technology is BANNED
• Data collection for marketing purposes is prohibited
• Publicly available policies required describing each use of surveillance technology

The ordinance follows a February 2026 council resolution directing the city manager to draft the regulation, which itself followed the June 2025 decision to end Austin's contract with Flock Safety (automatic license plate readers) after community privacy concerns. Council members added amendments during the April 24 vote.

This positions Austin among the most restrictive US cities on government surveillance technology. The TRUST Act reflects growing tension between technology deployment for public safety and civil liberties protections.

**June 22 update:** TRUST Act in implementation phase. Council on summer recess until July 23 (31 DAYS). Monitor for department technology approval requests when council returns.

**June 28 update:** TRUST Act in implementation phase. Council on recess until July 23 (25 DAYS). Monitor for department technology approval requests when council returns.

public_comment.instructions: Ordinance passed. Monitor implementation and any department technology approval requests that come to council.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: The TRUST Act is a significant win for privacy and government transparency — and it connects to the Waymo conversation happening April 29. Austin is establishing a framework for how the city interacts with emerging technology, from surveillance cameras to autonomous vehicles. The facial recognition ban and data disclosure requirements set a high bar that will shape future technology debates. For urbanists, this matters because technology deployment in public spaces affects the livability of dense neighborhoods. Worth monitoring how the ordinance interacts with APD's staffing challenges — if departments can't use certain surveillance tools, it strengthens the case for adequate human staffing.

issues.title: Texas House Passes Historic $100M Childcare Investment (HB 500)
issues.summary:
NEW (May 2026): The Texas House approved HB 500, a supplemental budget bill containing $100 MILLION for child care scholarships. The funding allows the Texas Workforce Commission to serve additional families in its Child Care Services program — critical given that 95,000 families are currently on the waiting list.

This builds on Governor Abbott's earlier signing of HB 117 and HB 4903 — HB 117 streamlines childcare regulations, while HB 4903 creates the Quad-Agency Childcare Initiative Commission to coordinate child care regulation across multiple state agencies.

LOCAL CONTEXT: Austin/Travis County's 'Raising Travis County' voter-approved initiative is in full rollout in 2026 — the fastest implementation of any childcare initiative of its kind in the country and the first in Texas. The $5M county transfer (March 2026) funds an interlocal agreement with the City of Austin for childcare quality improvement. Combined with the state's $100M investment, this creates a rare alignment of city, county, and state childcare funding.

The failure to provide adequate child care costs Texas $11.4 billion annually from productivity and revenue losses.

**June 22 update:** Raising Travis County now serving 9,402 children and youth through 25 contracts ($51.7M distributed) — the local complement to HB 500's $100M state investment. Demand still outpaces supply in nontraditional hours (early mornings, evenings, weekends). CDBG public comment through July 20; hearing July 14 at 9 AM.

**June 28 update:** Raising Travis County serving 9,402 children/youth through 25 contracts ($51.7M+ distributed) — the local complement to HB 500's $100M state investment. Demand still outpaces supply in nontraditional hours. CDBG comment through July 20; hearing July 14 (16 DAYS). Council on recess until July 23 (25 DAYS).

public_comment.instructions: State legislation. Monitor Texas Workforce Commission for implementation timeline and how Austin/Travis County families can access the new scholarships.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: A rare bipartisan win — $100M for childcare scholarships addresses a critical gap for Austin families. The 95,000-family waitlist is a concrete measure of the crisis. Combined with Raising Travis County's local investment, Austin is now at the intersection of city, county, AND state childcare funding. This creates an opportunity for the Abundance Project agenda: mapping where the new scholarship dollars flow, identifying remaining childcare deserts, and advocating for employer-side solutions to complement public investment.
