issues.title: Light Rail Transit Overlay Adopted — Transit-Supportive Zoning Along Phase 1 Corridor
issues.summary:
NEW (May 16, 2026): Austin City Council adopted the Light Rail Transit Overlay (Ordinance No. 20260516-005) to increase transit-supportive land uses and encourage affordable housing near the Phase 1 Austin Light Rail corridor and its Priority Extensions. The overlay creates new zoning incentives for dense, mixed-use development within walking distance of the 15 planned light rail stations stretching from 38th Street to Oltorf and east from Downtown to East Riverside. This is a critical companion policy to the Project Connect infrastructure investment — ensuring that the $8.23 billion transit system catalyzes housing production and walkable neighborhoods rather than remaining surrounded by parking lots and single-family-only zoning. The overlay responds to national evidence that transit investments without supportive land use policy fail to generate ridership or housing affordability benefits.

**June 22 update:** Overlay in effect since May 16. Project Connect legal status unchanged — Judge Shepperd must rule on AG Paxton's jurisdictional plea. Convention center funding ruling (June 18) provides positive precedent for public finance challenges. ATP continues property acquisition ($230M for 18 parcels). Council on recess until July 23 (31 DAYS).

**June 27 update:** Judge Shepperd has not yet ruled on AG Paxton's jurisdictional plea. ATP awarded $60M design-build contract to Austin Rail Constructors for pre-construction. Groundbreaking planned early 2027. Council on recess until July 23 (26 DAYS).

**June 28 update:** Judge Shepperd has not yet ruled on AG Paxton's jurisdictional plea. ATP awarded $60M design-build contract to Austin Rail Constructors. Groundbreaking planned early 2027. ⚡ Dog's Head TIRZ could fund a rail extension to Austin Airport per Urban Transportation Commission. Environmental Commission recommendation July 1 (3 DAYS). Council on recess until July 23 (25 DAYS).

public_comment.instructions: Overlay adopted. Monitor implementation and specific station-area plans as they develop.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: This is the land use policy that makes Project Connect's transit investment pay off. Without transit-supportive zoning, light rail stations get built next to parking lots. The overlay ensures density, mixed-use, and affordable housing incentives around all 15 stations — directly in your 78702 backyard along the East Riverside corridor. Combined with the Density Bonus Program (approved May 22), this creates a comprehensive framework for transit-oriented development. This is YIMBY policy at its best: using public infrastructure investment to unlock private housing production.

issues.title: 12 Shootings Across Austin May 17 — Public Safety Strain Exposed
issues.summary:
NEW (May 17, 2026): At least 12 shooting incidents occurred across Austin on May 17, leaving four people wounded. Two teenage suspects and a third suspect were taken into custody. The incidents spanned multiple neighborhoods and overwhelmed police response capacity. APD released a detailed timeline on May 18, identifying three suspects: Cristian Fajardo Mondragon (17), and two juveniles aged 16 and 15. The incidents were a mix of 'random and targeted shootings.' Investigators connected the cases through surveillance footage, ballistic evidence, stolen vehicle reporting, and coordinated intelligence sharing. APD confirmed there is no longer an ongoing threat to the public. This follows the March 1 mass shooting on Sixth Street that killed three and injured more than a dozen, and which the FBI investigated as potential terrorism. The spate of shootings underscores the ongoing strain on APD, which has 340 vacancies out of 1,816 authorized positions (19% vacancy rate). Despite recent progress — APD met its Priority 0/1 response time targets for the first time since August 2023 — the May 17 incidents demonstrate that recovery is fragile. The 157th cadet class (65 cadets) began April 21, but full staffing isn't expected until end of 2027.

**June 22 update:** 157th cadet class mid-training — graduation September 18. APD remains 300+ officers short. AISD campus police cuts from $205M budget could increase demand on APD patrol. Council on recess until July 23 (31 DAYS). Filing period for council races opens July 19 (27 DAYS) — public safety will be central to November campaigns.

**June 27 update:** 31+ homicides in 2026 as of June 24 — 5 homicides in 2 weeks in mid-to-late June (APD says unconnected). APD gained net 3 officers this year — first positive gain in years. Applications up 166%. Chief Davis projects full staffing by end of 2027. Council on recess until July 23 (26 DAYS).

**June 28 update:** 31+ homicides in 2026 as of June 24. APD gained net 3 officers this year — first positive net gain in years. Applications up 166%. Chief Davis projects full staffing by end of 2027. Council on recess until July 23 (25 DAYS).

The 340 APD vacancies and the March 1 mass shooting already framed this issue — May 17 adds urgency. This will likely dominate the 2026 council elections, with candidates in all five open seats forced to articulate positions on policing, gun violence prevention, and the hospital-based violence prevention programs. The budget context makes it harder: with a $26.4M FY2027 deficit, expanding public safety capacity competes directly with housing, homelessness, and infrastructure investments.

issues.summary:

LIHTC is the primary federal tool for financing affordable housing construction. Council support resolutions are required for competitive applications and signal local government backing. This comes alongside the $46.1 million approved to build and preserve affordable housing in Austin, spanning both new construction and rehabilitation of existing units. The LIHTC pipeline is critical infrastructure for the city's affordable housing goals — these six projects would add hundreds of income-restricted units across Austin.

issues.title: Citywide Density Bonus Program Approved — New Height/Affordability Framework
issues.summary:
NEW (May 22, 2026): Austin City Council approved sweeping new development rules in the citywide Density Bonus Program. The program allows developers to build 15, 30, 45, or 60 feet taller than current zoning permits in exchange for community benefits — primarily affordable housing units, but also wider sidewalks, trees, or payments into the Housing Trust Fund. The vote passed with only CM Duchen dissenting.

This replaces the controversial DB90 program, which gave a flat 90-foot height bonus and was criticized as too blunt. The new tiered approach calibrates height bonuses to neighborhood context: 15-foot intervals that better match the scale and needs of different areas. Combined with the Light Rail Transit Overlay (adopted May 16) and the HOME Initiative, Austin now has a comprehensive transit-oriented development framework.

CM Ryan Alter noted the program offers 'a range of options' with 'much better outcomes for everybody.' Critics warn it could bring large apartment buildings to neighborhoods where residents oppose them. Housing advocates are cautiously optimistic but want to see if the program actually generates development activity.

**May 29 update:** Downtown DBC (DDB400/DDB850) public hearing held May 28 — extends the density bonus framework into downtown with buildings up to 1,200 feet. Texas SB 840 removed FAR limits for multifamily/mixed-use. **May 30 update:** ⚡ The Real Deal published analysis (May 29) asking 'Austin's builders can now build taller. Will they?' — examining whether the new DBC framework will actually spur development activity. The question is whether developers take up the new height bonuses or whether market conditions, construction costs, and permitting timelines dampen uptake. Policy without developer uptake is just paper. Monitor for early DBC applications as the leading indicator of whether the framework produces housing.

CM Zohaib Quadri added an amendment to explore expanding the program south of Lady Bird Lake. Early DBC applications remain the key indicator of whether policy translates to housing.

issues.title: Dog's Head Annexation — 2,600-Acre East Austin Development Approved
issues.summary:
NEW (May 22, 2026): Austin City Council approved a 45-year development agreement to annex and develop more than 2,600 acres along the Colorado River known as the 'Dog's Head' tracts — roughly the size of downtown Austin. The deal allows for mixed-use development with the possibility of housing, commercial, and industrial use. City documents estimate the project could generate $3.5 billion in new property tax revenue over 30 years. Endeavor Real Estate Group is set to be the lead developer.

The vote was controversial — residents largely testified against the deal, which appeared on the council agenda with minimal advance notice and was not vetted by city commissions. Environmental groups raised concerns about one of the most ecologically sensitive stretches of urban river in Texas, as well as flooding risks. Critics also warned the 45-year lock-in prevents future councils from correcting course.

The tracts are hemmed in by a meandering section of the Colorado River east of US-183 and north of SH-130. The site's geography creates both development opportunity and environmental vulnerability. This is one of the largest single annexation/development actions in Austin's history.

**June 22 update:** 45-year development agreement in implementation phase. Council on summer recess until July 23 (31 DAYS). Monitor for specific development plans, environmental impact studies, and community benefit details when council returns.

**June 27 update:** ⚡ Travis County commissioners heard Endeavor Real Estate presentation June 26 on the Dog's Head development plan. Scale: 12,000 housing units, 3M sq ft retail, 1M sq ft industrial, 1M sq ft office. Current land value $17M; projected 2061 value $26B. Tax increment reinvestment zone (TIRZ) would reserve 80% of city tax revenue and 50% of county revenue for infrastructure. 20% affordable housing requirement, 260+ acres parkland, 6+ mile river trail. Commissioner Shea skeptical: 'This is a whole lot of trust me.' ⚡ KEY VOTES: Environmental Commission recommendation July 1 (4 DAYS); county TIRZ vote July 14 (17 DAYS); city council adoption July 23 (26 DAYS). If TIRZ not approved, Endeavor may disannex the property. Council on recess until July 23 (26 DAYS).

**June 28 update:** ⚡ KEY VOTES APPROACHING: Environmental Commission recommendation July 1 (3 DAYS); county TIRZ vote July 14 (16 DAYS); city council adoption July 23 (25 DAYS). Scale: 12,000 housing units, 3M sq ft retail, 1M sq ft industrial, 1M sq ft office on 2,600 acres. Current value $17M; projected 2061 value $26B. Commissioner Shea skeptical: 'This is a whole lot of trust me.' Commissioner Howard called TIRZ review process 'rushed.' Fortune 100 company reportedly driving Endeavor's accelerated timeline — may occupy 300 acres. If TIRZ not approved, Endeavor may disannex. 20% affordable housing requirement, 260+ acres parkland, 6+ mile river trail.

**June 29 update:** ⚡ Environmental Commission review July 1 (2 DAYS). Travis County scrutinizing TIRZ deal — commissioners questioned projections at June 26 presentation; county TIRZ vote July 14 (15 DAYS). City council adoption vote July 23 (24 DAYS). 2,600 acres; 12,000+ homes projected; 20% affordable (10% Endeavor, 10% city+others). $26B projected value by 2061. 6.5-mile public trail planned — initial 2-mile portion within 2 years of TIRZ adoption.

**June 30 update:** ⚡ Environmental Commission review TOMORROW (July 1) — June 17 commission meeting saw nearly 4 hours of public comment; environmental activists described TIRZ deal as 'dystopian.' Draft TIRZ plan expected after July 4 weekend. Travis County TIRZ vote July 14 (14 DAYS). City council adoption vote July 23 (23 DAYS). 2,600 acres; 12,000+ homes projected; 20% affordable. Environmental remediation alongside new construction on former industrial mining land.

**July 1 update:** ⚡ Environmental Commission review TODAY (July 1) — formal recommendation to Council on environmental protections. June 26: Travis County commissioners scrutinized the deal at a hearing ahead of TIRZ votes; intense opposition continues. Draft TIRZ plan expected after July 4 weekend. Travis County TIRZ vote July 14 (13 DAYS). City council adoption vote July 23 (22 DAYS). 2,600 acres; 12,000+ homes projected; 20% affordable.

**July 2 update:** Environmental Commission completed Dog's Head review July 1 — heard nearly 4 hours of public comment opposing the project on ecological grounds. Commissioner Brigid Shea publicly skeptical, citing 'a whole lot of trust me' from Endeavor Real Estate and insufficient detail about the mysterious 300-acre 'Project Toaster' tenant (undisclosed Fortune 100 company). Key concerns: Colorado River discharge impacts, environmental degradation, Tesla's past tax incentive noncompliance as cautionary precedent. Draft TIRZ plan expected after July 4 weekend. Travis County TIRZ vote July 14 (12 DAYS). City Council adoption vote July 23 (21 DAYS). 2,600 acres; 12,000+ homes; projected $3.5B property tax revenue over 30 years.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: 2,600 acres is a massive development footprint — larger than the 445-acre golf course rezoning. The $3.5B property tax revenue projection and mixed-use potential align with pro-development values, but the rushed process (no commission review, minimal public notice) and 45-year lock-in raise governance concerns. The environmental sensitivity of the Colorado River corridor adds complexity. This deal will shape East Austin for decades. Monitor implementation for housing density commitments, environmental protections, and community benefit provisions.

issues.title: NEW: Gas Peaker Plant — Council Approves ~$1B Plant, Oversight Guidelines
issues.summary:
NEW (May 2026): Austin City Council voted behind closed doors to approve Austin Energy's plan to build a new 400-megawatt natural gas peaker plant, with costs estimated around $1 billion. The closed-door vote sparked backlash from environmental advocates and government watchdogs who accused city leaders of shielding a controversial public decision from scrutiny. Because the city operates Austin Energy, state law allows City Council to privately vote on 'competitive matters' as the utility's governing body.

On May 29, Council approved oversight guidelines for the plant, directing Austin Energy to: (1) limit use of older gas peaker units at the Decker Creek facility to 'times of extreme scarcity,' (2) cap annual CO2 and nitrogen oxide emissions, and (3) follow new construction and operation standards. Council members said the rules will help reduce emissions and harmful local impacts.

Environmental groups (Solar Austin, Sierra Club) oppose the plant, arguing it contradicts Austin's climate goals and locks in fossil fuel infrastructure for decades. Austin Energy argues gas peakers are an 'unfortunate necessity' to maintain grid reliability as Texas faces growing electricity demand from data centers and population growth. The price tag could top $1 billion. The plant's location has not been formally disclosed.

This issue cuts across energy, environmental, fiscal, and transparency concerns — and will likely feature in November council races.

**June 22 update:** Oversight guidelines in effect since May 29 approval. Council on summer recess until July 23 (31 DAYS). Filing period for council races opens July 19 (27 DAYS) — the peaker plant remains a litmus test for November candidates on energy and climate policy.

All current council members refused to disclose their individual votes. TX Open Meetings Act §551.086 (enacted 1999) allows city-owned utilities to vote secretly on 'competitive matters.' Former CM Kelly said such votes 'always' occurred publicly during her tenure (2021-2025). 400 MW of new capacity; estimated ~$2,500/kW. Plant not operational until 2030. 14 possible sites identified — 9 of 14 in East Austin (all 10 existing peaker units already there). May 29: council passed emissions guidelines capping CO2 and NOx below pre-plant levels with extreme grid exemptions. Council on recess until July 23 (26 DAYS).

Council on recess until July 23 (25 DAYS).

issues.title: Austin Approves Sweeping Development Rule Changes — Lot Size, Missing Middle, Mixed-Use
issues.summary:
NEW (June 3, 2026): In a landmark week for Austin housing policy, the City Council approved multiple development rule changes in late May:

• **Minimum Lot Size** (May 26): Reduced from 5,750 sq ft to 1,800 sq ft citywide — the first change in over 80 years. Allows smaller, cheaper homes and enables more homes per block.

• **Density Bonus Program** (May 22): Developers can build 15-60 feet taller in exchange for affordable housing units or community benefits (wider sidewalks, green space). Only CM Duchen voted against. Replaces the controversial DB90 program.

• **Missing Middle Zoning** (May 2026): New zoning districts created for smaller housing types — townhomes, cottage courts, small multiplexes — that fill the gap between single-family homes and large apartment buildings.

• **Mixed-Use Zoning** (May 2026): New districts allowing commercial and residential development in tandem, enabling walkable neighborhood commercial corridors.

• **Dog's Head Annexation** (May 22): 2,600-acre annexation approved with a 45-year development agreement. 20% of housing must be affordable. Projected $3.5B in property tax revenue over the agreement term.

Combined with the HOME Initiative and Light Rail Transit Overlay (adopted May 16), Austin now has the most comprehensive pro-housing zoning framework of any major Texas city. Pew Research (March 2026) credited Austin's housing construction surge with driving down rents — these reforms extend that trajectory.

The Real Deal asked (May 29): 'Austin's builders can now build taller. Will they?' The answer depends on market conditions, interest rates, and developer uptake of the new DBC framework.

**June 4 update:** Implementation phase underway. Council on summer recess until July 23. Development community assessing DBC uptake — Real Deal (May 30) questioned whether Austin's builders will actually use new height bonuses.
